query: effect of epidural anesthesia on infant Apgar score during labor

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Treatment / Management -- Early versus delayed pushing
retrieved_docs.content: A large meta-analysis involving 5,445 patients with neuraxial analgesia from 12 RCTs conducted between 1979 and 2018 found that early pushing, on average, shortened the 2nd stage of labor by 46 min compared to delayed pushing. However, early pushing resulted in 27.5 more min of pushing. Early pushing was also linked to lower rates of chorioamnionitis (9.1% versus 6.6%) and low umbilical cord pH (2.7% versus 1.3%), with variations in definitions of low pH across trials. [37] Rates of spontaneous vaginal birth, assisted vaginal birth, cesarean birth, intrapartum fever, endometritis, postpartum hemorrhage, episiotomy, 3rd- and 4th-degree perineal lacerations, 5-min APGAR scores less than 7, and neonatal respiratory morbidity were similar regardless of when pushing began.

retrieved_docs.title: APGAR Score -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: Any trained healthcare professional may calculate the Apgar score depending on the situation, including: Neonatologist Pediatrician Nurse practitioner Family physician Midwife Nurse Respiratory Therapist

retrieved_docs.title: APGAR Score -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the physiological criteria used for calculating the Apgar score. Interpret the clinical relevance of the Apgar score. Determine the limitations of the Apgar score. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and patient outcomes when evaluating newborns using the Apgar score. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: APGAR Score -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Apgar scores may vary with gestational age, birth weight, maternal medications, drug use or anesthesia, and congenital anomalies. Several components of the score are also subjective and prone to inter-rater variability. Thus, the Apgar score is limited because it provides somewhat subjective information about an infant’s physiology at a point in time. Apgar score alone should not be interpreted as evidence of asphyxia, and its significance in outcome studies, while widely reported, is often inappropriate. Resuscitation should always take precedence over calculating a clinical score.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Management of Ex Utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) and Fetal Surgery -- Clinical Significance -- Fetal Monitoring and Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: Profound uterine atony is achieved by volatile anesthetics at 1 to 1.5 MAC with a bolus of 4 to 6 g of magnesium sulfate just during skin incision followed by 2-4 gm/hr continuous infusion. Nitroglycerine may be supplemented if more relaxation is required. When the fetus is exposed, fetal anesthesia and analgesia are achieved using a combination of fentanyl 10 to 20 mcg/kg, vecuronium 10- 20 mcg/kg, and atropine 10- 20 mcg/kg as an intramuscular injection, often referred to as the ‘fetal cocktail.’ A fetal pulse oximeter is attached, and continuous fetal echocardiography is performed. The uterus is filled with warm saline to avoid any uterine contraction, which is constantly monitored by the obstetrician.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Management of Ex Utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) and Fetal Surgery -- Issues of Concern -- 1. Altered Maternal Physiology and Maternal Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: The parturient coming for fetal surgery usually undergo extensive testing and counseling before the procedure and are usually healthy, fall under ASA I or II physical status. However, altered physiology related to pregnancy affects anesthetic management and may pose specific risks. The risks are briefly summarized in the table below.

retrieved_docs.title: Ex utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) Procedure -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The required equipment for an EXIT procedure can vary significantly based on the evaluation performed during the workup phase and the planned EXIT type. Given the mother's open surgical site, the airway equipment must be kept sterile in these circumstances. Generally, all EXIT procedures require the following equipment: Anesthesia circuits for both mother and fetus with invasive maternal blood pressure monitoring Foley catheter Two large-bore peripheral intravenous lines Type-and-crossed blood for possible maternal transfusion in the room Possible epidural catheterization Ultrasound machine with a sterile sleeve Cesarean delivery surgical set Electrocautery Uterine stapling device Amnioinfusion Fetal resuscitation bay Optional adjacent operating room set up ready to receive infant

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Management of Ex Utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) and Fetal Surgery -- Issues of Concern -- 1. Altered Maternal Physiology and Maternal Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: Regardless of the anesthetic plan, the parturient is kept nil per oral for the procedure. They receive Metoclopramide and Sodium citrate for aspiration prophylaxis. Some sedation with midazolam and fentanyl may be helpful to relieve maternal anxiety and to facilitate epidural placement. Left lateral uterine displacement is employed to decrease the risk of supine hypotension syndrome from aortocaval compression. Maintenance of maternal blood pressure is important to maintain placental blood flow and fetal perfusion. Fluids and vasopressors can be used. However, fluids should be cautiously used because of the risk of pulmonary edema from absorption of a larger amount on the irrigation fluid from the surgical site and some procedures from amnioinfusion. [2] The choice of vasopressors can be ephedrine or phenylephrine, as they have been found safe and do not cause clinically significant compromise in the placental flow and fetal acidosis. [3] Perioperative pain management decreases the risk of premature uterine contraction and premature labor and improves maternal satisfaction. So multimodal analgesia is usually offered to them, including the epidural.

retrieved_docs.content: Apgar scoring at 1 and 5 minutes The general condition of the neonate Vital signs of the newborn Umbilical cord pH Arterial blood gases of the newborn

retrieved_docs.title: APGAR Score -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Apgar scoring has no known contraindications in the evaluation of newborns. However, in certain situations (eg, an infant who needs to be paralyzed or undergo surgery at birth), the score may not reflect the underlying physiology.

retrieved_docs.content: Anesthesia

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- Introduction -- Second Stage Protraction and Arrest
retrieved_docs.content: For nulliparous patients: second stage duration of more than 3 hours without an epidural or 4 hours with an epidural For multiparous patients: second stage duration of more than 2 hours without an epidural or 3 hours with an epidural Longer durations may be appropriate with reassuring maternal and fetal statuses and continued descent of the fetal presenting part.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural (Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural anesthesia is routinely offered to laboring parturients to achieve pain management during labor, delivery, and the immediate postpartum period. Consequently, neuraxial analgesia can improve exhaustion and overall satisfaction in the obstetric population. An interprofessional team includes the anesthesiologist, surgeon, obstetrician, and specialty-trained nurse. Communication among the team will optimize patient care. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: APGAR Score -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians, advanced practitioners, midwives, respiratory therapists, and nurses play pivotal roles in Apgar scoring, with consistency in scoring crucial to mitigate inter-rater variability. Interprofessional team members are each instrumental in assessing neonates and promptly communicating any changes in Apgar scores to the attending clinician. Health professionals each play a crucial role in documenting scores at 1 and 5 minutes and working with other clinicians in initial resuscitative measures for low-scored neonates. Interprofessional communication ensures findings are shared with the mother and family, facilitating collaborative care planning. Physicians, advanced practitioners, midwives, and nurses should address family concerns, provide education leaflets, and emphasize patient education for managing neonates with low Apgar scores. This collaborative approach fosters optimal patient-centered care, enhancing outcomes and safety through effective team performance.

retrieved_docs.title: Cesarean Delivery -- Technique or Treatment -- Pain Management and Enhaced Recovery after Surgery Protocols
retrieved_docs.content: Pain management for cesarean deliveries involves a combination of preoperative anesthesia and an enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) protocol, which spans the preoperative, perioperative, and postoperative periods. [42] A regional anesthetic, such as a spinal or epidural block, is the preferred modality of surgical anesthesia and should be administered promptly before surgery. Regional anesthetics help minimize fetal exposure to agents that could suppress respiration or adversely affect the newborn's transition.

Both anesthesia‑related cardiac and CNS complications are classified under “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” highlighting their impact on maternal care after delivery, and they are identified as narrower subsets of pregnancy complications, emphasizing that they arise in the context of gestation and delivery. Fetal disorder (“اضطراب جنيني”) is recorded as a parent of pregnancy complications, underscoring the contribution of intrinsic fetal abnormalities to maternal obstetric risk. The late‑effect category “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس” (late effect of pregnancy, birth, and postpartum complications) is classified as a type of pregnancy complication, linking long‑term sequelae to the acute obstetric event. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2081
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications that arise from anesthesia or other sedation administered during labor and delivery, especially when accompanied by a postpartum complication, are categorized as “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother.” This broader category is further classified under the concept of “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس,” which refers to the delayed effects of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications. Consequently, such cardiac events are understood as part of the spectrum of maternal complications that can influence postpartum care and require specific attention in the management of the mother after delivery.

Recognizing how each element relates to the central concept of labor aids clinicians in anticipating potential risks, selecting appropriate interventions, and optimizing outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2227
retrieved_docs.content: Central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication are identified as a more specific subset within the broader category of pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل). Fetal disorder (اضطراب جنيني) is also classified as a type of pregnancy complication, indicating that abnormalities affecting the fetus fall under the umbrella of complications that can arise during gestation. Premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر) likewise belongs to the group of pregnancy complications, reflecting its significance in obstetric outcomes. Together, these entities illustrate how diverse obstetric and perinatal issues—ranging from anesthesia‑related central nervous system effects to fetal abnormalities and early membrane rupture—are organized under the overarching concept of pregnancy complications, which is essential for comprehensive clinical assessment and management.

Fetal disorders (اضطراب جنيني) are linked to pregnancy complications as a child entity, reflecting the impact of maternal health on the fetus. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2040
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication represent a more specific subset within the broader category of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complications). Likewise, اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is also classified under مضاعفات الحمل, indicating that fetal disorders are considered a type of pregnancy complication. Together, these relationships show that both anesthesia‑related cardiac events during labor and fetal disorders are encompassed by the overarching concept of pregnancy complications, underscoring the need for careful obstetric monitoring and management of these distinct yet related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل) represent a broad clinical category that includes several more specific conditions and classifications. Congenital disorder (اضطراب جنيني) is identified as a child entity of pregnancy complications, indicating that fetal anomalies are considered a type of obstetric complication. The late‑effects category of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications (التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس) is also classified under pregnancy complications, emphasizing that sequelae occurring after delivery remain part of the overall obstetric risk profile. Within this late‑effects group, the category “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother” is placed, signifying miscellaneous maternal problems that influence postpartum care. Both cardiac complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication and central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication are classified as “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” and each is described as having a narrower relationship to the overarching pregnancy complications. This hierarchical organization clarifies how specific anesthesia‑related cardiac and neurological events during labor and delivery fit within broader obstetric complication frameworks, aiding clinicians in accurate classification, risk assessment, and comprehensive management of maternal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 971
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication and Central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication are both specific, narrower subsets of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complications). Each of these anesthesia‑related complications is classified as “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” a category that in turn is classified as التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس (the delayed effects of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications). اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is also placed under مضاعفات الحمل, indicating that fetal disorders are considered a type of pregnancy complication. تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes) is identified as a form of مضاعفات الحمل, and it is clinically associated with a low transverse cesarean section. Together, these relationships illustrate how various maternal and fetal conditions—ranging from anesthesia‑related cardiac and central nervous system events to premature membrane rupture and fetal disorders—are organized within the broader framework of pregnancy complications and their delayed postpartum effects, informing clinical classification and management strategies.
